original_abstract: The cryogenic separation of noble gas mixtures is an energy-intensive process, creating a need for efficient solid-state adsorbents capable of operating at ambient temperatures. While metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) containing polarizing functional groups are predicted to be effective for Xe/Kr separation, experimental data regarding gas-framework interactions remain scarce. Here, we report the synthesis and characterization of SBMOF-2, a new microporous calcium-based MOF that exhibits high selectivity for Xe over Kr. At 298 K, SBMOF-2 demonstrates a Xe/Kr selectivity of approximately 10 and a Xe adsorption capacity of 27.07 wt%. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction analysis reveals that this selectivity originates from unique pore geometries, specifically cages lined with phenyl rings and polar -OH groups that provide strong adsorption sites for the polarizable Xe atoms. We further evaluated the separation performance through both computational and experimental breakthrough studies. Under practical conditions, Kr was found to elute significantly earlier than Xe, confirming the material's potential for industrial nuclear fuel reprocessing and air separation. Computational comparisons indicate that the Xe capacity and selectivity of SBMOF-2 are competitive with the highest-performing unmodified MOFs currently known, such as NiMOF-74.
humanized_abstract: Cryogenic extraction of noble gases from air and nuclear fuel mixtures is highly energy intensive. Hence, there is an urgent need for solid-state adsorbents that can separate the noble gases at ambient conditions. Although porous materials such as zeolites and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have shown great promise, detailed studies on the interaction of gases with these materials are scarce. In this context, we report on a new microporous calcium-based MOF denoted as SBMOF-2 that shows enhanced affinity towards Xe as compared to Kr. At ambient temperature, Xe/Kr selectivity is observed to be around 10 and Xe adsorption capacity is found to be 27.07 wt%. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction studies reveal that the cages present in the pores of the SBMOF-2 contain phenyl rings and -OH groups, which facilitate the strong adsorption of Xe atoms on account of their high polarisibility. Breakthrough curves of SBMOF-2 obtained from computational as well as experimental studies reveal the early elution of Kr in comparison with Xe, which affirms the application of SBMOF-2 in nuclear fuel reprocessing as well as air separation. Comparison of Xe capacity and Xe/Kr selectivity of SBMOF-2 with unmodified MOFs reveals that the performance of SBMOF-2 is competitive with that of the highest Xe capacity and selectivity values reported so far, which is observed for NiMOF-74.
